home.title: Electric Car Database
home.heroSubtitleEv: Browse the database, save relevant EVs and start your comparison directly from the catalog.
home.heroSubtitleIce: Find suitable ICE vehicles, keep your shortlist in view and send them straight into the cost comparison.
home.resultsDescriptionEv: Scan the database, switch the view mode and save candidates you want to compare next.
home.resultsDescriptionIce: Review the models calmly, switch the view and mark vehicles for the next comparison step.
home.evSubtitle: Find matching EVs and send them directly from card, list or detail view into the calculator.
home.iceSubtitle: Review matching combustion cars and move them straight into the cost comparison without breaking the flow.
home.searchPlaceholder: Search by brand, model or category
home.flowHint: Every card, list row and detail view can send a vehicle directly into the calculator.

steps.findDescription: Browse the database and use whichever view lets you scan the vehicles fastest.
steps.compareDescription: Send vehicles from card, list or detail view straight into the calculator without losing context.
steps.decideDescription: Use TCO, break-even and cost drivers to make the decision financially, not just technically.

landing.meta: Advisor, comparison and cost understanding in one flow
landing.sub: Start guided in the advisor, jump straight into the comparison or browse the database if you prefer exploring on your own.
landing.pathsTitle: How the website guides you to a choice
landing.advisorCardDescription: Later you will define what counts as good, okay or irrelevant. The advisor should only evaluate once you click search.
landing.calculatorCardDescription: Send vehicles straight into the TCO calculator and review break-even, cost structure and scenarios.
landing.browseCardDescription: Browse EVs and ICE vehicles freely, save favorites and move into comparison or the calculator afterwards.
landing.truckFeatureMeta: E-trucks as their own homepage area
landing.truckFeatureDescription: The E-truck area has its own homepage entry, separate from the regular car flow. That gives fleet topics, knowledge and tools a clearly dedicated space.
landing.truckFeatureAria: Illustration of an electric pickup used as a placeholder for the E-truck area

truck.subtitle: This is a dedicated E-truck area with clear paths: first understand relevance, then frame economics and operations, and finally choose suitable models.
truck.heroPanelTitle: Three paths, depending on your question
truck.heroPanelDescription: The area is deliberately split into three entry points so you land in the right topic directly instead of scrolling through one long mixed page.
truck.groupHubTitle: Choose the right entry point into the E-truck area first
truck.groupHubDescription: When someone opens the truck area, not everything should appear at once. These three main paths lead into the topic first, and only then into deeper content.
truck.understandingOverviewTitle: Three things to understand about E-trucks first
truck.understandingOverviewDescription: Before comparing models, charging power or cost numbers in detail, a short framing helps: why E-trucks matter, where they fit well and why timing matters now.

relevance.title: E-trucks are not just a climate topic, but a cost and future topic
relevance.description: Market pressure, CO2 targets and toll advantages all push the topic at the same time. That makes the transition more commercially and strategically relevant than a few years ago.

fit.title: Not every operation fits equally well, but many profiles are already realistic
fit.description: Predictable routes, return-to-depot logic and clear charging windows already make E-trucks plausible in many urban and regional applications.

timing.title: The question is no longer only 'if', but 'when and where first?'
timing.description: For many companies, now is the right time to define the first credible pilot operations and build real operating experience.

truck.architectureTitle: How the E-truck area will be built
truck.architectureDescription: The page should not just show data. It should guide companies from orientation to vehicle selection. That is why the area gets three dedicated working directions.

advisor.title: Truck advisor
advisor.description: An advisor for use case, range, charging windows, depot charging, payload and fleet requirements.

database.title: Model and comparison database
database.description: A dedicated data layer for E-truck models, technical metrics, charging capability and commercial cornerstones.

knowledge.title: Information and strategy hub
knowledge.description: Curated knowledge on market dynamics, TCO, tolls, charging infrastructure, range, safety and rollout into operations.

truck.advisorPreviewDescription: Before individual models are compared, the truck advisor should first understand operational reality. These four questions form the first working structure.

useCase.title: Where does the truck actually run?
useCase.description: Urban logistics, regional transport and long haul need very different range, charging and timing assumptions.

route.title: How predictable is the route?
route.description: Daily mileage, return-to-depot logic, weather and topography buffer, and the desired safety margin all shape model suitability early.

charging.title: What charging base really exists?
charging.description: Depot charging, public fast-charging need, grid connection and available charging windows need to be understood before spec comparisons make sense.

operations.title: Which limits must not break?
operations.description: Payload, body type, toll leverage, shift logic and TCO targets define whether a model fits not just technically, but commercially.

truck.advisorPreviewNote: Like the passenger-car advisor, the truck advisor only evaluates after a deliberate click instead of recalculating on every input change.
truck.findVisualTitle: From use case to a plausible truck shortlist
truck.findVisualDescription: The decision starts with route logic, charging setup and operating reality, not with spec sheets. That is what makes the vehicle shortlist meaningful.
truck.findVisualAria: Stylised illustration of an electric truck with glowing charging and decision signals

range.label: Not only range, but the real tour logic determines fit.

charging.label: A truck only fits once charging works in practice.

decision.label: Spec sheets only become reliable when tolls, energy and operations are included.

truck.findFlowDescription: First define the use case, then narrow the suitable models, and only then validate the business case.

profile.title: Define the use case
profile.description: Daily mileage, payload, return-to-base logic and range buffer narrow the relevant vehicle space first.

compare.title: Compare suitable models
compare.description: Only vehicles that are operationally plausible should move into deeper data and model comparisons.

business.title: Validate the business case
business.description: At the end, tolls, charging, GHG and infrastructure show how the real economics shift.

truck.findFlowNote: This keeps the area logical: use case first, then model fit, then the business case.

berater.title: Turn the use case into suitable trucks
berater.description: Set route, charging and operating limits and review the shortlist on the right.

modelle.title: Compare the current truck data base directly
modelle.description: Compare the current data base directly by range, payload and charging logic.

truck.findHelpTitle: When each entry path makes sense
truck.findHelpDescription: The deeper answers live here so the overview and work surfaces can stay compact.

advisor.question: When should I start directly in the truck advisor?
advisor.answer: The advisor is the right entry when the route profile, charging window, payload and trailer requirement need to be clarified first. It helps turn the use-case logic into a robust operational shortlist.

models.question: When is the model overview enough on its own?
models.answer: The model overview fits when you already know what you are looking for and want to inspect the current data base directly. It is the faster path for comparing range, battery, payload and charging logic.

tco.question: When should the economic check follow?
tco.answer: The TCO check only makes sense once the operational shortlist is stable. Before that, it is often still unclear whether charging base, use case and trailer requirements really fit the trucks being considered.

workspace.title: Truck advisor as an operations console
workspace.description: Define the operation on the left. The right side keeps only the trucks that still fit it operationally.
workspace.sidebarDescription: Build the draft here first. The right side only reorders after Apply search.

fields.returnToDepot: Return to depot on the same day

useCase.title: Define the use case
useCase.description: First clarify in which operational corridor the truck is supposed to run.

route.title: Frame route and range
route.description: Daily kilometres, buffer and return logic define the operational base.

charging.title: Frame charging and infrastructure
charging.description: Depot charging, fast charging and grid status all shape the first fit picture.

operations.title: Set the operating limits
operations.description: Payload and similar constraints keep the first matches realistic.

refinement.title: Refine operations and tech
refinement.description: Vehicle format, trailer requirement and charging standard make the shortlist more operationally reliable.

results.title: Control the result view
results.description: Search and sorting organize the right side without recalculating the operational fit itself.

summary.filtersText: This many inputs currently shape the fit picture.
summary.matchesText: These models currently fit well or very well.
summary.nextText: This many trucks are currently hidden because range, payload or charging base do not fit.
summary.catalogText: The current base already covers {{brands}} manufacturers.

workspace.resultsTitle: Which trucks currently fit your draft best?
workspace.resultsDescription: This shortlist only keeps the models that currently fit your operational draft best.

results.leadAhead: currently leads the next model by {{gap}} points.
results.leadSolo: is currently the only plausible match in your setup.
results.noWatchouts: No critical issues in the first fit picture right now.
results.emptyTitle: No plausible truck match right now
results.emptyDescription: Check the charging base, daily kilometres and payload first. In the current draft, all available models are excluded.
results.emptySearchTitle: The current search hides all visible matches
results.emptySearchDescription: There are plausible models for your draft, but the current search or sort setup is not showing any right now.
results.excludedNote: {{count}} models are currently hidden because range, payload or charging base do not fit the draft.

reasons.rangeTight: range is close to the limit
reasons.payloadTight: payload is close to the limit
reasons.fastChargeReady: fast charging can stabilize the case
reasons.ccsEnough: CCS is sufficient for the current draft
reasons.gridMissing: grid status should still be clarified

business.title: How economically viable does this truck fit look?
business.description: After the operational shortlist comes the first business-case framing. This is not the full calculation yet, but it already makes the next commercial step visible.

ready.title: Business case looks connectable
ready.description: The operational fit is strong, and infrastructure no longer looks like the main blocker. It now makes sense to move into a real TCO review.

pilot.title: Pilot looks commercially plausible
pilot.description: The shortlist looks credible, but operations or infrastructure should still be stress-tested before scaling.

review.title: Refine the operating logic first
review.description: There are usable paths, but range, charging or trailer logic are not strong enough yet for a clean business case.

blocked.title: No viable business case yet
blocked.description: As long as no plausible operational model remains, the commercial assessment should not start yet.

highMileage.title: High daily mileage strengthens the toll lever
highMileage.description: With many kilometres per day, the CO2-differentiated toll advantage becomes a meaningful positive cost driver.

midMileage.title: Tolls and energy can already work well
midMileage.description: At a medium daily profile, toll savings and operating costs can already start to offset the higher entry price.

selectiveMileage.title: Economics stay more use-case dependent
selectiveMileage.description: At lower utilisation, the business case becomes more sensitive. Charging readiness, operational discipline and incentives then matter more.

stableBase.title: No dominant business blocker is visible
stableBase.description: In the current draft, no single blocker dominates. That suggests the next step is to connect tolls, charging costs and TCO assumptions.

missingChargeBase.title: Without a charging base the business case collapses
missingChargeBase.description: If neither depot charging nor reliable fast charging is viable, the commercial logic becomes unstable very quickly.

gridMissing.title: The grid issue remains the main blocker
gridMissing.description: If depot charging is planned but the grid status is unresolved, the business case remains too uncertain operationally and commercially.

mcsGap.title: Charging ambition and truck capability still diverge
mcsGap.description: If MCS is preferred but the truck charges more conservatively, the real break-window and recharge case should be checked in detail.

rangeTight.title: Range remains commercially sensitive
rangeTight.description: A tight range fit can force unplanned charging windows and extra time pressure, which can hurt commercial viability indirectly.

payloadTight.title: Payload is close to the limit
payloadTight.description: If payload demand has almost no buffer, a technically fitting truck can still become commercially inflexible in daily operation.

chargeWindowTight.title: Charging window remains too narrow
chargeWindowTight.description: Tight charging windows increase the risk that charging power, break structure and cost logic will not line up cleanly.

noOperationalFit.title: The operational base is not viable yet
noOperationalFit.description: Before tolls, GHG or energy prices are discussed, there needs to be a plausible truck fit in the operation itself.

buildBusinessCase.title: Now connect the full economics layer
buildBusinessCase.description: Next, tolls, charging price, infrastructure and rollout assumptions should be applied to this shortlist.

clarifyGrid.title: Clarify grid connection and charging base first
clarifyGrid.description: Before running TCO, verify whether the intended charging base is realistic and available in time.

pricePublicCharging.title: Price public fast charging properly
pricePublicCharging.description: If the fit relies heavily on public charging, the business case should be mirrored early with realistic fast-charging costs.

testCcsFallback.title: Test the MCS expectation against the truck reality
testCcsFallback.description: A short scenario check helps here: is CCS enough in practice, or does the long-haul case become too tight commercially?

runPilot.title: Move forward with a controlled pilot
runPilot.description: The fit looks promising, but a limited pilot is the cleanest way to validate operations and economics together.

reworkProfile.title: Refine the use case first
reworkProfile.description: Before deepening the numbers, the draft should become more precise on range, charging or trailer need.

tco.title: What does the first TCO view of the frontrunner look like?
tco.description: For the current frontrunner we now run a simplified 5-year preview against a diesel equivalent. This shows whether tolls, energy, GHG and maintenance are already able to carry the higher upfront cost.
tco.advantageText: already comes in below the diesel equivalent across this five-year preview.
tco.extraCostText: still remains above the diesel equivalent in this five-year preview.
tco.breakEvenBefore: The extra investment would break even after roughly {{years}} years.
tco.breakEvenAfter: Under these assumptions the extra investment would not fully break even within five years.
tco.assumptions: Simplified preview across {{years}} years. The current assumption set uses {{depotShare}} depot charging and {{publicShare}} public fast charging. Before a final decision, mirror this preview against real fleet, energy and toll assumptions.

summary.annualBenefitText: How strong the current running-cost delta looks per year.
summary.capexDeltaText: How far the truck currently sits above the diesel equivalent.
summary.chargingMixText: Depot versus public fast-charge share in the current preview.
summary.yearlyDistanceText: Derived from daily km and use-case profile.

fields.energyCostText: Based on consumption, charging mix and power-price assumptions.
fields.tollBenefitText: The CO2-differentiated toll effect remains one of the strongest levers.
fields.thgText: Mirrored as a rough N3 preview using the current quota logic.
fields.maintenanceText: Simplified difference between an e-truck and diesel in operation.

truck.findCompareTitle: Choose the right entry into Find & Compare
truck.findCompareDescription: Start in the advisor from the use case or inspect the models directly.

advisor.title: Derive suitable vehicles from the use case
advisor.description: Not only specs, but also tour type, charging window, payload and operational rules shape the first shortlist.

database.title: Compare models in a structured way
database.description: The database puts charging power, battery, range, weights, body options and other model data side by side cleanly.

tco.title: Frame TCO, tolls and charging assumptions
tco.description: After model selection, the economic layer shows how tolls, energy, GHG and infrastructure shift the business case.

truck.findCompareNote: The economic follow-up sits directly behind the advisor once the operational shortlist is in place.
truck.findModelsTitle: Current truck data base at a glance
truck.findModelsDescription: All available E-trucks at a glance. Use it to quickly check which models are already part of the current base.
truck.findModelsNote: {{models}} models from {{brands}} manufacturers currently make up the active truck data base.
truck.topicsTitle: The first content blocks for high-value information
truck.topicsDescription: From your material, we pull the highest-value topics to the front first. That gives us a solid information area before we expand every detail.

verstehen.description: This is the big-picture area: why E-trucks matter, what market logic is driving them, and in which operational profiles the technology already fits well today.
verstehen.cardDescription: Get a quick view of market logic, use cases and technology.
verstehen.panelTitle: Why E-trucks are becoming relevant now
verstehen.panelDescription: This area bundles market context, strategic signals and real-world use cases into a first clear orientation.

wirtschaftlichkeit-betrieb.description: This is the operational core: which cost drivers matter, how tolls and GHG help, and what depot charging, regulation and operating reality actually mean.
wirtschaftlichkeit-betrieb.cardDescription: TCO, charging, rollout and regulation for real fleet decisions.
wirtschaftlichkeit-betrieb.panelTitle: Economics come from multiple levers at once
wirtschaftlichkeit-betrieb.panelDescription: The purchase price alone does not decide the business case. This area combines TCO, tolls, charging infrastructure and rollout into one operating view.

finden-vergleichen.description: This area brings the truck advisor, model overview and economic follow-up together in one clear decision surface.
finden-vergleichen.cardDescription: Advisor, model view and economics as one working area.
finden-vergleichen.panelTitle: From use case to a robust model decision
finden-vergleichen.panelDescription: This area connects the truck advisor, model overview and commercial follow-up in a single workflow.

market.title: Market development and suppliers
market.description: An overview of why E-trucks are becoming economically relevant now and which manufacturers are shaping the market.
market.pointOne: Germany as a key lead market for heavy E-trucks
market.pointTwo: Big Seven, new models and competitive pressure
market.pointThree: How to frame scaling, market share and long-haul readiness

economics.title: Economics instead of gut feeling
economics.description: A structured view of extra CapEx, toll benefits, GHG quota, energy prices and operational savings.
economics.pointOne: Toll savings as the strongest economic lever
economics.pointTwo: GHG quota as an additional revenue stream
economics.pointThree: Lifecycle cost thinking instead of upfront price only

infrastructure.title: Infrastructure as the key constraint
infrastructure.description: The page should explain why depot charging, grid connection and MCS determine operational success.
infrastructure.pointOne: Depot charging as the main charging scenario
infrastructure.pointTwo: Plan grid connection and load management early
infrastructure.pointThree: Classify CCS and MCS logically by use case

operations.title: Range, operations and qualification
operations.description: A module for real-world range, payload rules, DGUV qualification, fire safety and driver training.
operations.pointOne: Operational capability instead of brochure values
operations.pointTwo: Weight allowance, 44-ton rule and dispatch planning
operations.pointThree: Safe operation through training and standards

truck.signalsTitle: Four signals that make E-trucks strategically relevant right now
truck.signalsDescription: This first orientation condenses market movement, toll logic, scaling pressure and infrastructure into four clear reasons why E-trucks belong on the decision agenda already today.

momentum.title: Heavy E-trucks are growing despite a weak overall market
momentum.description: According to the material you provided, registrations of heavy E-trucks above 16 tonnes in Germany rose to 1,398 units in 2025. That is real momentum even while the overall truck market softened.

scale.title: Market share is still small, but scaling is now mandatory
scale.description: The share of heavy E-trucks in sales remains low. That is exactly why the current phase matters so much for OEMs, hauliers and charging infrastructure players.

toll.title: The toll system is the strongest short-term economic lever
toll.description: Heavy diesel trucks carry high toll costs, while zero-emission trucks were exempt through the end of 2025 and remain strongly advantaged afterwards.

infrastructure.title: Success depends on depot charging and grid connection
infrastructure.description: Most charging events are expected to happen at the depot. That makes charging infrastructure an operational core issue rather than a side topic.

truck.economicsTitle: Frame the most important economic levers first
truck.economicsDescription: Before comparing individual models, the area needs a clear economic logic. These four levers explain why heavy-duty decisions are almost always made through TCO thinking.

capex.title: The entry price is high, but the delta is visible
capex.description: For heavy tractor units, the extra cost versus diesel is currently often in the range of roughly €200,000. The traction battery remains the biggest cost block.

toll.title: Tolls can offset a large share of the extra CapEx
toll.description: At 100,000 km of annual mileage, the toll advantage for heavy vehicles becomes a major economic driver compared with diesel in Germany.

thg.title: The GHG quota can further support the business case
thg.description: For heavy N3 vehicles, the GHG premium can become a meaningful additional revenue stream. At the same time, both timing and value remain market-dependent.

opex.title: Maintenance drops, energy becomes a management topic
opex.description: Electric drivetrains remove many classic maintenance burdens. At the same time, profitability depends heavily on how much energy is charged cheaply at the depot versus expensively in public.

truck.economicsNote: Note: tolls, GHG premiums, incentives and energy prices are time-sensitive. Values like these should always be read with a review date and context.
truck.opsOverviewTitle: Three things decide this page first
truck.opsOverviewDescription: Before opening the deeper modules, this short view helps frame the page: where the biggest lever sits, where the biggest risk sits, and which question should be answered first.

lever.title: Tolls and energy logic shift the TCO picture most strongly
lever.description: The biggest shift rarely comes from one component alone. It usually comes from the combination of toll advantage and low-cost depot electricity.

risk.title: Charging infrastructure and grid connection delay rollouts more often than the truck itself
risk.description: Many projects do not fail on vehicle technology, but on lead times for power capacity, transformer work or robust charging windows.

decision.title: The first question is not 'Which truck?' but 'Which route first?'
decision.description: A commercially sensible transition almost always starts with a viable route, not with the technically most exciting vehicle.

truck.opsSplitTitle: Start with the question you actually need to answer
truck.opsSplitDescription: Instead of stacking cost logic, charging reality and regulation on one long mixed page, this area is now split into three clearer perspectives.

business-case.title: Make the business case reliable first
business-case.description: This view focuses on extra CapEx, toll savings, GHG revenue, operating costs and the four questions that should be answered before ordering.

laden-betrieb.title: Plan charging and operations properly
laden-betrieb.description: This view checks how pilot rollout, depot charging, charging windows, grid connection and day-to-day operations fit together.

regulatorik.title: Frame tolls, incentives and targets
regulatorik.description: This view focuses on the rules that increase pressure, change timing and influence the business case beyond the vehicle itself.

truck.rolloutTitle: How a company should prepare the transition structurally
truck.rolloutDescription: Instead of switching the whole fleet at once, almost everything points to a staged, data-driven rollout. These four steps form the first working pattern.

one.title: Pilot routes before full rollout
one.description: Start with 1:1 replacements on predictable short-haul and regional routes and gather real operating data before scaling the fleet.

two.title: Secure grid connection early
two.description: Involve the grid operator early, because higher capacity and new transformer stations can require one to two years of lead time.

three.title: Build the energy mix strategically
three.description: Think depot charging, load management, PV and energy management as one system so low-cost electricity becomes a durable advantage.

four.title: Make TCO and route simulation the standard
four.description: The list price alone should never drive the decision. TCO, tolls, GHG, charging windows and real routes need to define the business case.

truck.rolloutNote: Future check: incentives, toll rates and public high-power charging availability will keep changing. The final knowledge area should therefore maintain all such content with clear time references.
truck.regulationTitle: Tolls, GHG revenue and incentives are part of the investment logic
truck.regulationDescription: For heavy E-trucks, some of the biggest business differences do not sit in the vehicle alone. They sit in toll treatment, GHG revenue, the incentive landscape and the timing logic around all of them.

toll.title: CO2-differentiated tolls massively shift the business case
toll.description: Heavy diesel trucks carry high per-kilometre toll costs, while zero-emission trucks were exempt through the end of 2025 and remain strongly advantaged afterwards. This lever affects every real route.

thg.title: The GHG quota can become a meaningful extra layer in the business case
thg.description: Heavy N3 vehicles can earn noticeable premiums through their estimated electricity consumption. At the same time, market pricing, filing partner and deadlines remain critical.

incentives.title: Incentives now need much stronger regional checking
incentives.description: After the central KsNI programme stopped for new applications, state-level and regional programmes became much more important. For companies that means checking location, timing and funding windows carefully.

compliance.title: Regulation pushes OEMs and operators toward earlier decisions
compliance.description: EU fleet targets, CO2 pressure and the rollout of new infrastructure mean that waiting can itself become a strategic risk. Timing therefore becomes part of the business case.

truck.regulationNote: Important: this area deliberately separates core knowledge from current status. Toll rates, incentives, GHG revenues and deadlines can change quickly.
truck.chargingTitle: Which charging types make sense for which job today
truck.chargingDescription: Not every charging level solves the same problem. For the E-truck area, the key is to classify systems by use case, dwell time and operational role.

ac.title: AC charging
ac.description: AC is mainly a depot and dwell-time topic. For large long-haul batteries it is too slow, but for calm overnight windows it can be very economical.
ac.time: Typical: around 10–15 hours for a large battery
ac.useCase: Best for: overnight charging when time matters more than speed

ccs.title: CCS
ccs.description: CCS already covers many structured regional use cases. It is a useful bridge between simple depot charging and very fast public charging.
ccs.time: Typical: around 4–8 hours for a large battery
ccs.useCase: Best for: depot, day-edge windows and predictable charging slots

hpc.title: CCS high power
hpc.description: High-power CCS matters when the truck needs faster top-ups on route without yet reaching megawatt charging levels.
hpc.time: Typical: around 1.5–2.5 hours for a large battery
hpc.useCase: Best for: line haul, faster intermediate charging and regional long-distance

mcs.title: Megawatt Charging System
mcs.description: MCS is the real unlock for heavy long-haul operations. It is what makes large batteries economically chargeable inside operational break windows.
mcs.useCase: Best for: motorway corridors, long haul and pause-based charging

truck.chargingNote: Important: real charging time depends not only on charger power, but also on battery size, charge curve, temperature and available grid capacity.
truck.checklistTitle: These four questions should be clear before ordering
truck.checklistDescription: These four items move the topic from orientation into a credible pre-check. They form a sensible minimum structure before any pilot or vehicle order.

route.title: Which real route is the first E-truck candidate?
route.description: Start with a real tour including mileage, topography, dwell times and safety reserve instead of a generic wish profile.

charging.title: Where and when will the truck actually charge?
charging.description: Without a clear charging window, depot logic and backup scenario, even a technically suitable vehicle remains operationally weak.

grid.title: Can the required electrical capacity actually be secured?
grid.description: Grid connection, load management and likely upgrade lead times must be clarified early or the whole rollout timeline shifts.

economics.title: Is the business case based on real assumptions?
economics.description: Tolls, GHG, charging-price mix, maintenance and actual route logic should be reviewed together before fleet quantities are decided.

truck.opsFaqTitle: The detail questions that usually appear after the first numbers
truck.opsFaqDescription: The main page only shows the core logic. The deeper points live here for the second review round.

timing.question: When is it worth moving into a real TCO review?
timing.answer: As soon as the use case, range and charging base look operationally plausible. Only then do tolls, energy price, GHG revenue and infrastructure assumptions become reliable enough for a fleet decision.

chargingBase.question: Why must the charging base be clarified before scaling?
chargingBase.answer: Without a secured charging base, even a strong model fit can fall apart in day-to-day operations. Depot charging, grid connection, charging windows and a fast-charge backup all influence whether the economics can really be achieved.

pricing.question: Which cost assumptions deserve the closest scrutiny?
pricing.answer: Above all public fast charging, real annual mileage, toll leverage and infrastructure costs. These four points often move the business case more than small differences between individual truck models.

truck.useCasesTitle: Where E-trucks already fit well and where the hurdles are still higher
truck.useCasesDescription: Not every use case is equally mature. This first framing helps think about E-trucks through operational fit rather than through specs alone.

urban.title: Urban and municipal operation
urban.description: This is often the clearest current fit: predictable routes, many stops, recuperation and regular return to depot all support the drivetrain well.
urban.strength: Strength: predictable routes and strong recuperation
urban.focus: Check first: dwell times, charging windows and municipal route patterns

regional.title: Regional distribution and line haul
regional.description: Regional profiles are often the best bridge into scale. They combine higher daily output with still manageable charging planning.
regional.strength: Strength: good balance between range and predictability
regional.constraint: Constraint: public intermediate charging may become relevant
regional.focus: Check first: depot charging plus a robust backup charging scenario

longhaul.title: Heavy long-distance transport
longhaul.description: Long haul is becoming increasingly realistic, but it is most dependent on battery size, charge rate, MCS rollout and dispatch quality.
longhaul.strength: Strength: strong toll lever and fast technology progress
longhaul.constraint: Constraint: public high-power infrastructure and tight time windows
longhaul.focus: Check first: real route, MCS outlook and operational margin

truck.comparisonTitle: Why the E-truck is currently the most plausible next step
truck.comparisonDescription: This framing does not replace a complete market study, but it helps explain quickly why battery-electric trucks currently offer the strongest operational perspective in road freight.

bev.title: Strongest perspective for many predictable road operations
bev.description: Direct electricity use, toll advantages and predictable depot charging make BEV trucks the most realistic transformation path for many fleets today.

diesel.title: Still the current reference, but under growing cost pressure
diesel.description: Diesel remains the starting point in many fleets, but it is steadily losing ground because of tolls, CO2 pressure and future strategy requirements.

hydrogen.title: Interesting for special cases, but currently weaker operationally and commercially
hydrogen.description: For broad road freight use, hydrogen is currently usually more expensive and far less infrastructure-ready than battery-electric solutions.

truck.comparisonNote: For many fleets, battery-electric is already the most plausible entry point, as long as use case, charging setup and TCO are assessed together.
truck.faqTitle: Four questions that usually come up first
truck.faqDescription: These short answers help frame the most common reservations before going deeper into models, infrastructure or business-case details.

fit.question: Do E-trucks already fit many real routes today?
fit.answer: Yes, especially where routes are predictable, vehicles return to depot regularly and charging windows can be organised reliably.

charging.question: Does every operation need megawatt charging from day one?
charging.answer: No. For many urban and regional profiles, depot charging or structured CCS charging is enough. MCS becomes critical mainly in heavy long-haul use.

cost.question: Is the higher purchase price automatically a deal breaker?
cost.answer: Not necessarily. Only when toll savings, energy cost, GHG revenue, maintenance and real mileage are considered together does the business case become clear.

timing.question: Should companies wait for later model generations?
timing.answer: Not as a rule. For many operators it is more valuable to start with a credible pilot now and build operating experience than to wait for a supposedly perfect moment.

truck.nextTitle: The groundwork is now in place
truck.nextDescription: Next we can deepen one of the three strands on purpose: truck advisor, model database or high-quality knowledge modules with real content.

advisor.title: Advisor
advisor.subtitle: The first advisor version now guides the user through switchable criteria, good and okay thresholds and a deliberate search click toward suitable vehicles.
advisor.statusSearch: Scoring only starts after clicking Search.
advisor.statusToggle: Every criterion can be switched on or off individually.
advisor.statusFlow: Suitable vehicles then move directly into comparison and calculator.
advisor.setupTitle: Choose relevant values and apply them deliberately
advisor.setupDescription: First pick the drivetrain. Then only activate the criteria that really matter to you and define a good and okay threshold for every active field.
advisor.criterionDisabledHint: This criterion is currently switched off and does not influence scoring or filtering.
advisor.invalidThresholds: Please enter sensible good and okay thresholds.
advisor.exactMatchHelp: This criterion only rewards exact matches.
advisor.multiSelectHelp: You can pick multiple values. Every selected value counts as an exact match.
advisor.ruleExact: Only exact matches for {{value}} are recommended.
advisor.ruleExactMultiple: Only exact matches for these values are recommended: {{values}}.
advisor.ruleHigher: Good from {{good}}, okay from {{ok}}, still visible until {{weak}}. Below that it is excluded.
advisor.ruleLower: Good up to {{good}}, okay up to {{ok}}, still visible until {{weak}}. Above that it is excluded.

basis.title: Basics
basis.description: Price, body style and first core values for the quick shortlist.

praxis.title: Real-world use
praxis.description: Daily life, family use and utility for the actual job.

technik.title: Tech
technik.description: Range, efficiency, charging and drivetrain details for fine tuning.

advisor.searchDescription: {{count}} active criteria will only be applied to vehicles after clicking Search.
advisor.resultsDescription: Results are calculated only from the applied criteria. Vehicles below the exclusion threshold no longer appear here.
advisor.searchPending: Set up your criteria and deliberately start the search with a click.
advisor.noResults: No suitable vehicles were found for this combination of criteria.
advisor.resultReasonFallback: Ahead because the active criteria fit better overall.
advisor.resultsLimitNote: Showing the first {{shown}} of {{total}} matching vehicles.
advisor.principlesDescription: Phase 1 sets up the entry point and the rules before the actual scoring engine and criteria forms are implemented.

principles.criteriaDescription: You define two thresholds for relevant metrics. These create points, warnings and exclusions.
principles.toggleDescription: If a value does not matter to you, you switch it off. Then it no longer influences scoring or filtering.
principles.searchDescription: The advisor should not recalculate live on every input. Criteria are only applied and sorted after clicking Search.

advisor.flowDescription: The advisor should lead into comparison and then into the cost calculator instead of being three disconnected tools.

flow.step1Description: Set drivetrain, profile and active criteria.
flow.step2Description: Save suitable vehicles and inspect them side by side.
flow.step3Description: Read TCO, break-even and cost drivers in the calculator.

advisor.browseTitle: Until the scoring logic is ready
advisor.browseDescription: The database remains the fastest way to discover vehicles already now and collect them for the next step.

calc.title: Cost Calculator
calc.pageSubtitle: Compare EVs and ICE vehicles within the same product language as the catalog and review the key assumptions in context.
calc.setupDescription: Choose your vehicles, define the usage profile and review the most important cost assumptions before jumping into the result.
calc.resultsDescription: As soon as both sides are complete, the calculator shows the most important conclusions first and the deeper explanations afterwards.

sections.vehicleDescriptionEv: Pick the electric vehicle from the database and review the imported base data.
sections.vehicleDescriptionIce: Choose an ICE vehicle from the database or enter it manually.
sections.usageDescription: These values apply to both vehicles and determine how strongly the running costs matter over time.
sections.costsDescriptionEv: Set purchase price, charging prices and charging shares for home, public AC and fast charging.
sections.costsDescriptionIce: Set purchase price and fuel cost for the baseline comparison.
sections.expertDescriptionEv: Fine-tune financing, maintenance, insurance, residual value, subsidies, THG, wallbox and charging losses.
sections.expertDescriptionIce: Fine-tune financing, maintenance, insurance, tax and residual value.

hints.chargeProfile: Charging shares can be entered freely. The calculator normalises them to 100% internally if needed.
hints.drivingProfile: Driving profile shares can be entered freely. The calculator normalises them to 100% internally if needed.
hints.drivingProfileNeutral: Normal keeps consumption neutral and intentionally adds no extra effect to the calculation.
hints.fuelPrice: Use your realistic average price here instead of only today's fuel price.

validation.fixSlotBeforeResults: Please correct the highlighted inputs before the calculator can show a reliable result.
validation.positiveRequired: Please enter a value greater than 0.
validation.nonNegativeRequired: Please enter a value greater than or equal to 0.
validation.percentRange: Please enter a percentage between 0 and 100.
validation.lossRange: Please enter a charging loss between 0 and 50%.
validation.grantExceedsPurchasePrice: The grant currently exceeds the purchase price and will be capped in the calculation.
validation.profileShareTotalZero: At least one driving profile share must be greater than 0%.
validation.profileShareNormalized: The driving profile shares add up to {{total}}%. The calculator normalises them to 100% internally.
validation.chargeShareTotalZero: At least one charging share must be greater than 0%.
validation.chargeShareNormalized: The charging shares add up to {{total}}%. The calculator normalises them to 100% internally.
validation.leaseIgnoresResidual: Residual value is currently not included when financing type is lease.

results.title: How the total cost really differs
results.subtitle: This view highlights the most important cost drivers over {{years}} years at a glance.
results.cheaperThan: {{winner}} is cheaper than {{loser}} over the selected period.
results.breakevenDescription: {{name}} catches up with the cost gap after {{years}} years.
results.noBreakevenDescription: {{name}} stays the cheaper option over the selected period.
results.sectionProfileTitle: How does your driving profile change consumption?
results.sectionProfileDescription: This view shows how your mix of city, rural and highway driving shifts the effective consumption of each vehicle.
results.profileNarrativeNormal: In Normal mode the consumption stays neutral and is not shifted by a special driving profile.
results.profileNarrativeEvCity: A city-heavy usage profile generally benefits this EV and lowers its effective consumption.
results.profileNarrativeEvRural: A strong rural share keeps this EV close to its base consumption.
results.profileNarrativeEvHighway: A high highway share noticeably increases this EV's effective consumption.
results.profileNarrativeIceCity: A city-heavy usage profile pushes this combustion car's effective consumption up.
results.profileNarrativeIceRural: A strong rural share is usually the most efficient case for this combustion car.
results.profileNarrativeIceHighway: A highway-heavy profile keeps this combustion car closer to its base value than city traffic.
results.sectionBreakEvenTitle: When does the cost comparison flip?
results.sectionBreakdownTitle: Which blocks drive the monthly cost?
results.sectionBreakdownDescription: This chart shows which cost categories create the biggest monthly burden.
results.driverNarrativeValueLoss: This mainly reflects acquisition cost and residual value over the holding period.
results.driverNarrativeEnergy: This is driven primarily by consumption, driving profile and energy price.
results.driverNarrativeFinancing: This reflects differences in loan cost or lease payments.
results.driverNarrativeRunning: This combines maintenance, insurance and tax.
results.driverNarrativeInfrastructure: This is mostly wallbox and installation cost.
results.sectionScenarioTitle: How robust is the result under different assumptions?
results.sectionScenarioDescription: Test annual kilometres as well as electricity and fuel prices here. The result cards react immediately to your changes.
results.scenarioAnnualKmHint: This scenario value is applied to both vehicles.
results.scenarioElectricityHint: Applies to home, public AC and fast charging at the same time.
results.scenarioFuelHint: Applies to the average fuel price per litre.
results.scenarioDisabledEv: There is currently no EV in the active comparison.
results.scenarioDisabledIce: There is currently no combustion car in the active comparison.
results.sectionTableTitle: All cost positions at a glance
results.sectionTableDescription: The table includes the full breakdown, including residual value and THG income.

calc.fixInputs: Please complete or correct the highlighted inputs to see results.
